text: The patient is a sixty-two year old female with one day history of lower abdominal pain. CT scan of the abdomen suggests acute Meckel's diverticulitis without perforation.
Upon entering the abdominal cavity, the surgeon observes inflammatory adhesions surrounding the Meckel's diverticulum.
The surgeon uses scissors and electrocautery to carefully dissect and remove inflammatory adhesions surrounding the Meckel's diverticulum.
The surgeon identifies an area of perforation in the small bowel, visually inspecting the affected tissue.
The surgeon identifies the base of the Meckel's diverticulum and carefully frees it from surrounding tissue using surgical instruments.
The surgeon places an endoscopic stapler around the base of the Meckel's diverticulum, ensuring the small bowel lumen remains unobstructed.
The surgeon frees the last attachment of the Meckel's diverticulum, completing its separation from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon places the specimen into an endo bag and removes it from the surgical field, ensuring containment and safe extraction.
